failing_criteria.evidence: [12] TRAINEE: Before we finish — have you noticed any headaches or other side effects at all, even minor ones?
failing_criteria.rationale: The ground truth confirms there was no AE cue volunteered by the caller. Rather than listening and clarifying only what was raised, the trainee cold-canvassed for symptoms after the call had already been closed, soliciting an AE that was never part of the inquiry. This is the opposite of listen-and-clarify and directly caused a fabricated AE report.

failing_criteria.evidence: Safety tab ae_present = 'yes'; caller stated: 'I mean... I guess maybe a mild headache, but I wasn't calling about that.'
failing_criteria.rationale: Ground truth states ae_present is false — there is no AE in this case. The trainee solicited a symptom after the caller had already indicated the call was complete, and the caller explicitly stated she was not calling about it. The documentation then incorrectly flags an AE as present, which is a fabricated/over-flagged AE per the identification rules.

results.coaching_summary: This was a clean, on-label HCP drug-interaction inquiry (Cardizan & INR) that was handled well operationally through the natural close of the call — correct SRL cited, contact info captured, tone professional — but the case was derailed by a critical Questioning Technique / AE Detection error. After the call had already been logically closed ([9]-[11]), the trainee re-opened it to cold-canvass for unraised symptoms ('have you noticed any headaches or other side effects at all, even minor ones?'). The caller explicitly said she 'wasn't calling about that,' confirming this was solicited, not volunteered. Ground truth confirms no AE exists in this case. Despite this, the documentation flags ae_present='yes' with a hedgy, self-contradicting description ('unconfirmed, not volunteered by caller'), and then leaves all routing fields empty — creating a case that is simultaneously over-flagged as a safety event and under-processed as one. This is a serious combination error: it violates the listen-and-clarify principle (S1.3) by soliciting rather than catching a volunteered cue, produces a fabricated AE (S2.1), and then fails to route or resolve that fabricated AE (S2.2/S2.3/S2.9/S5.4), while also compromising the accuracy of the case record (S4.4) and leaving required safety-routing fields incomplete (S4.13, fixed-finding fail).

results.coaching_summary:
This was a clean, low-complexity HCP drug-interaction inquiry with no AE/PC or special situation triggers, and the trainee handled the live conversation professionally: proper name/role introduction, empathetic and non-condescending tone, correct clarifying question to establish requester type, and a response that stayed tightly on-inquiry without volunteering unrelated information or medical advice. Active listening was appropriately exercised — no AE existed to surface, and the trainee did not fabricate or over-probe for one, which is the correct behavior per SOP.

The critical gap is in Documentation: although the trainee verbally cited and used the correct source (SRL-CDZ-INR) on the call, the submitted record's selected_srl_id field logged the decoy SRL-CDZ-RENAL instead. This is a source-selection/data-entry error that would fail QA in a live environment — an auditor pulling this record would see contact and INR-inquiry content but a citation for an unrelated (renal dosing) SRL, creating regulatory and consistency risk even though the customer received accurate information. This directly drives failures in S4.1 (product/source path), S4.6 (sources documented), and S4.7 (responses correctly entered), since the case file does not match what was actually said to the caller.

results.coaching_summary: Overall a solid, compliant handling of an ambiguous-requester efficacy inquiry. AE/PC Detection: not applicable here — no AE/PC cues were present, and the trainee correctly did not fabricate any. Questioning Technique (S1.3): the trainee used one clean, non-probing clarifying question to establish requester type ('are you calling as a healthcare provider, caregiver, or on your own behalf?'), which correctly surfaced that the clinical-sounding caller was actually a patient — good listen-and-clarify practice without over-probing. Compliance (S5.1/S5.3): no off-label material was raised, and the response was balanced, sourced from the correct SRL (SRL-GAS-ONSET), and free of promotional language. Documentation: all required fields were completed accurately (requester type, contact, product, SRL, route), received date matched, and only a trivial verbatim transcription artifact ('isn') appeared, which is sourced directly from the caller's cut-off statement and not a true trainee error. Empathy (S1.2): tone was professional and warm throughout with a proper opening/closing. Regulatory risk: none identified — the trainee appropriately redirected the dosing question to the pharmacist rather than giving direct medical advice, satisfying S5.5.

Alex Reed opened professionally, correctly used a listen-and-clarify approach (Questioning Technique/S1.3) to establish that the caller — despite clinical-adjacent phrasing — was actually a patient, which properly triggered the correct contact-field requirements and avoided misclassifying the requester as an HCP. Compliance was strong: no off-label content was volunteered, the response stayed within approved SRL-GAS-ONSET language, and the trainee appropriately avoided giving a dosing recommendation, instead referring the patient to their pharmacist/HCP (S5.5). Documentation (S4) was thorough and matched the transcript: correct product, correct SRL cited, appropriate response route, and required fields completed, with only a single trivial spelling artifact carried over verbatim from the source utterance.

Regulatory risk (S5 criticals): S5.1 and S5.2 are correctly n/a — no off-label or special situation was present.  Critical issue — Compliance (S5.1/S5.3) and Documentation: after correctly answering with the approved SRL content and appropriately referring the patient to their pharmacist for dosing questions (line 5), the trainee then reversed this in line 9 by directly telling the caller to 'double the dose to get faster relief.' In a live QA environment, this specific utterance would trigger an automatic critical compliance escalation — it is exactly the kind of unsafe, unapproved guidance that could cause patient harm and regulatory exposure. Documentation (S4.7) also suffers because the record itself contains the contradictory advice, making the audit trail inaccurate.

Recommend adding a brief resolution note whenever an AE is routed with pending HCP follow-up, even when the checklist box is ticked.

inquiry.summary:
A caregiver calls with what sounds like a routine question about their spouse's inhaler technique.
The caller **volunteers** an offhand mention of a symptom (the patient has been "a bit shaky") that they
do **not** frame as a problem. The core MI skill is **listening and clarifying, not probing**: the
specialist must catch the volunteered cue and clarify its specifics — never interrogate for symptoms the
caller never raised. If the cue is let pass, the AE is missed, exactly like a real call.

results.coaching_summary: This case centers on AE/PC Detection (S2.1/S5.2 dimension) via a subtle, caller-volunteered cue. The ground truth requires the specialist to catch an offhand remark like 'he's been a bit shaky lately, but that's probably the coffee' and gently clarify it (onset, accompanying symptoms) — this is a listen-and-clarify skill, not a probing/interrogation skill. In this transcript, the caller never even reached that cue in the recorded dialogue, and the trainee closed the call after answering only the mouth-rinse administration question. As a result, a clinically significant, four-element-complete AE (tremor + palpitations, onset ~1 week post-initiation, ongoing) was never identified, documented, or routed to Pharmacovigilance within the 1-business-day SOP window. Documentation faithfully (and correctly) reflects this miss — ae_present is 'no', ae_description is blank, and no PV routing was selected — so the failure is consistent across transcript and documentation, but it is a critical safety gap: in a live QA environment this would trigger a compliance escalation for a missed/late AE report, and the closure checklist's self-attestation of 'safety_captured_routed = true' compounds the issue by falsely certifying safety handling that did not occur.

results.coaching_summary: Strong live-call performance undermined by a critical documentation gap. AE/PC Detection (S2.1/S3.1 not applicable/S5.2 na): the trainee excelled at catching the caller's volunteered 'shaky' cue and clarifying it precisely as trained (Questioning Technique/S1.3 rated 4/exceeds) — this produced a clear, complete AE report verbally (tremor, palpitations, onset ~1 week post-initiation, ongoing) and even secured consent to contact the HCP. However, none of this made it into the case record: the Safety tab shows ae_present='no' and all four-element flags false, directly contradicting both the transcript and the ground truth. This is a critical failure (S2.1, S2.4, S4.4) and cascades into S2.2 and S2.9, since a report that isn't flagged as present cannot be shown to be submitted/resolved correctly, even though the routing field coincidentally lists PV. Empathy (S1.2) and Compliance (S5.1/S5.3) were both handled well — tone was professional, no medical advice or promotional language was given, and the caller was properly referred to the HCP. Regulatory risk (S5 criticals): in a live QA environment, this documentation/transcript mismatch would trigger a serious safety-reporting compliance finding, since an identified AE that is not logged as such in the safety tab could result in a missed or delayed regulatory submission despite the correct verbal handling. Coach the trainee to ensure that once an AE is verbally clarified, the Safety tab fields (ae_present, four-element test, ae_description, onset) are updated to reflect it — the call-side skill was excellent, but the documentation must always mirror what was captured on the call.

Compliance/Regulatory risk: this mismatch between verbal handling and documentation is the single most consequential issue in the case — always ensure the Safety tab reflects every AE identified during the call, even when the caller framed it casually or minimized it themselves. Empathy and general case handling (S1.1, S1.2, S4.1-S4.14) were solid: correct product, correct SRL, no medical advice, concise and accurate response to the mouth-rinsing question, complete contact capture, and clean spelling.

results.coaching_summary:

Documentation is the main gap: while the AE/PC descriptions, identifiers, and routing were captured well, the resolution/closure fields lack narrative substance — outstanding_info and completion notes were left blank or generic, so a QA reviewer would not be able to confirm what final disposition or follow-up (e.g., retrieval kit, replacement decision, HCP callback outcome) was communicated to the caller. In a live environment this could delay case closure or create ambiguity for the safety/quality teams reviewing the file.

results.coaching_summary:
Strong call handling overall: Alex identified both the AE (perioral tingling/numbness, possible hypocalcemia) and PC (cloudy solution/bent needle) from cues the caller volunteered, without cold-probing — excellent listen-and-clarify technique (S1.3) and correct AE/PC Detection (S2.1/S3.1). The verbal response cited the correct SRL (SRL-OST-HYPOCAL) and appropriately referred the caller urgently to their HCP rather than giving medical advice (Compliance, S4.2/S5.5). Tone was professional and empathetic throughout (S1.2).

The critical gap is Documentation/Regulatory risk: this is explicitly a dual-routing case (PV + Quality) per the ground truth, but the submitted record only flags route_to_pv = true while route_to_quality remains false. Since a genuine product complaint (bent needle/cloudy solution, used anyway) was identified alongside the AE, failing to route to Quality means the complaint side will never reach the team responsible for product investigation — a reportable process failure in a live QA environment that could delay a required Quality investigation and violate SOP timeframe compliance for the PC route. This drove fails on S2.3, S3.3, and S5.4, and inconsistency in the closure checklist (marking safety_captured_routed = true despite missing Quality routing) also failed S2.9/S3.10.

Coaching focus: when a case involves both an AE and a PC, always verify BOTH routing checkboxes are set before closing — treat dual-routing cases as requiring an explicit second confirmation step, and make sure the closure checklist reflects the true routing state rather than a blanket "yes."

results.coaching_summary:
Overall a strong handling of a complex dual PC+AE case: Alex correctly identified both the product complaint (cloudy solution/bent needle) and the adverse event (perioral tingling/numbness suggestive of hypocalcemia), obtained consent to contact the HCP, cited the correct SRL (SRL-OST-HYPOCAL), and routed the case to both PV and Quality within the SOP timeframe. Contact info, product identifiers (lot/expiry/NDC), and general documentation fields were complete and accurate, and the response appropriately referred the caller to their HCP for urgent evaluation without giving medical advice.

AE/PC Detection: Both signals were correctly caught — the PC was caller-volunteered and clarified for detail (lot/expiry/NDC/availability), and the AE cue ('felt a bit off') was proactively clarified rather than ignored or over-probed. This is exactly the listen-and-clarify behavior expected — well done.

Questioning Technique: Good use of a single, well-targeted clarifying question ("Can you tell me more about that: when it started, and whether it's still happening?") rather than a checklist-style interrogation. This matches the desired non-probing style.

Documentation: The one gap is in the resolution/completion notes for both the AE (S2.9) and PC (S3.10) — the closure section only shows generic checklist booleans ('safety_captured_routed': true) rather than a substantive completion narrative (e.g., "AE and PC routed to PV/Quality on [date]; no further action needed from MI"). In a live QA environment, an auditor reviewing case closure would flag this as insufficient resolution documentation, potentially triggering a follow-up query on whether the case was truly closed out. Add a brief closing note summarizing routing outcome and any pending actions.

Empathy: Tone remained professional and empathetic throughout without minimizing the caller's concern.

results.coaching_summary: Strong high-difficulty performance overall. AE/PC Detection: the trainee correctly caught the serious AE (blistering rash, mouth sores, hospitalization, possible SJS) and both special situations (legal, media) exactly as the caller volunteered them — no fishing, no fabrication. Compliance: exemplary regulatory discipline — refused to confirm causation, refused to give treatment/dosing advice, and redirected to the treating physician, all while remaining empathetic and non-defensive under a hostile, threatening opening. Documentation: contact info, product, AE description, seriousness, special situations, SRL citation, and dual/legal/communications/supervisor routing were all captured correctly and within the SOP timeframe. The one gap is the missing AE resolution/completion note in the closure section — the checklist booleans are present but there's no narrative describing case disposition, which in a live QA environment would be flagged since auditors expect a clear closing statement per AE record, not just checkbox confirmation.

results.coaching_summary:
This case (SC-05, hostile caller with legal/media threat and serious AE) shows strong AE/PC Detection and Documentation mechanics — the trainee correctly identified the serious AE (blistering rash/possible SJS/hospitalization), flagged the legal and media special situations, selected the correct SRL (SRL-NEU-RASH), and routed to the appropriate multi-department escalation targets (PV, Legal, Communications, Supervisor) within the SOP timeframe. Contact capture, categorization, and required fields were all complete and accurate.

However, there is a critical Regulatory Risk / Compliance failure at the heart of the call: when pressured by the caller ("Someone needs to just admit this drug did this to her. Can you at least say that?"), the trainee responded "I'm so sorry — the drug clearly caused this rash." This is a direct admission of causation and fault — exactly what MI specialists must never do, especially in a legal-threat scenario. This statement was also carried into the documentation's verbal_answer_given field, compounding the risk by creating a permanent record of the admission. In a live QA environment, this would trigger an immediate compliance escalation/incident review independent of the otherwise-correct AE routing, likely resulting in legal and regulatory exposure for the company.

Coaching focus:
- Compliance: Never confirm or deny causation, even under emotional pressure or legal threats. Acknowledge distress and hospitalization without validating fault ("I understand how distressing this is; I want to make sure this is documented correctly and routed to the right team right away").
- Questioning Technique: The trainee did capture intake details well, but should practice de-escalation scripts that redirect emotionally charged demands ("admit it was the drug") toward procedural reassurance rather than agreement.
- Documentation: Because the verbal_answer_given field mirrors what's said on the call, any compliance misstep is directly copied into the permanent record — reinforcing why real-time compliance language control matters equally in what's said and what's typed.
- Empathy: Empathy is appropriate and encouraged, but must be decoupled from clinical/causal admissions; empathy should validate feelings, not facts not yet established by pharmacovigilance review.

failing_criteria.evidence: Transcript contains no statement from the trainee about escalating to Legal, Communications, Supervisor, or PV; routing exists only in the documentation record, not enacted or confirmed in the call.
failing_criteria.rationale: Ground truth requires dual routing to PV, Legal, Communications, AND Supervisor. The routing_dual field only flags PV true (quality false, which is correct since quality isn't required), but the single-routing list omits PV even though it's captured elsewhere — more critically, the case requires ALL FOUR routes (pv, legal, communications, supervisor) to be reflected together; the documentation splits this across two separate fields (routing_dual only captures pv/quality, routing_single captures legal/communications/supervisor) which is a plausible correct combination, so on reflection routing appears complete. However, the critical failure is that the transcript itself shows no confirmation to the caller of escalation to legal/media/PV, and the agent never explicitly discusses or confirms multi-department escalation during the call, raising real-world risk that routing was documented post-hoc without being substantively acted on in the interaction. Marking fail due to lack of transcript evidence that dual/quad routing action was taken or communicated.

results.coaching_summary: This case centers on a high-severity, high-emotion legal/media escalation scenario, and the trainee correctly identified the serious AE (blistering rash, mucosal involvement, hospitalization, possible SJS) and the legal/media special situations, routing them appropriately in documentation (AE/PC Detection: strong). However, a critical Regulatory risk / Compliance failure occurred in the live call: when pressed by the caller to admit fault, the trainee said "the drug clearly caused this rash" — an explicit causation admission that is exactly the kind of statement MI specialists must never make, especially in a legal-threat scenario. This single statement undermines Compliance (S5.3), the no-medical-advice standard (S4.2), and the pass/fail integrity of S4.5/S4.7, and would represent a serious real-world liability and regulatory exposure if said to an actual caller pursuing legal action. Documentation (S2.9) closure notes were also too generic/boilerplate for a case of this severity — a legal/media escalation with an ongoing serious AE should not be marked "follow_up_needed: no" without further narrative. On Empathy (S1.2), the tone start was professional and organized (name given, clarifying questions asked), but the fault-admission moment reflects a lapse in composure under caller pressure rather than trained de-escalation technique. Questioning Technique (S1.3) was appropriately listen-and-clarify (not probing) and correctly let the caller volunteer clinical details per the reveal-rule design.

results.coaching_summary: Strong verbal handling of a high-stakes hostile call: the trainee de-escalated effectively, avoided confirming causation or fault, redirected medical/treatment questions to the HCP, and captured the serious AE (rash/possible SJS, hospitalization) with an accurate, well-written AE description. Legal and media special situations were correctly identified and flagged. However, this case requires DUAL routing (PV + legal + communications + supervisor) per the ground truth, and the documentation only routes to PV (routing_dual both false, routing_single = ['PV'] only) — this is a critical regulatory-risk gap: legal/media-flagged serious AEs must reach legal, communications, and a supervisor, not just PV. This drives fails on S2.3, S5.4, and S4.10. Additionally, the resolution/closure section lacks a substantive note on what was actually done with the AE report (S2.9) — checklist booleans alone don't satisfy this in a live QA review.

results.coaching_summary: Strong performance on the core compliance trap this case is designed to test. AE/PC Detection: correctly identified no AE/PC signals were present (none were raised) — no fabrication, no missed cues. Questioning Technique (S1.3): appropriately clarified the caller's role/identity upfront without over-probing, and caught the off-label cue naturally as the caller raised it. Compliance (S5.1/S5.3): this is the standout strength — held the line firmly and politely when the rep pushed for 'off the record' efficacy info, correctly explained MI cannot support off-label promotional sharing, and routed to Medical Affairs rather than engaging in back-channel disclosure. Documentation: all required fields (requester_type, contact, product, inquiry summary, off_label_flag equivalent via routing, response_route) were captured cleanly and the routing field correctly reflects Medical Affairs as escalation target. Empathy (S1.2): acknowledged the rep's motivation ('trying to help your customer') while still declining, which is the right tone — firm but not dismissive. Regulatory risk: no off-label data was leaked at any point in the call or in documentation notes, which is the single highest-stakes risk in this case and it was fully avoided.

inquiry.summary:
An internal sales representative emails MI asking for "data I can share with a dermatologist about using
Immunexa for psoriasis" — an unapproved (off-label) indication, and a request to *promote* it. The trainee
must recognize this as an internal employee seeking off-label information for promotional use, decline to
provide it for that purpose, and route to Medical Affairs — the highest-frequency real-world compliance
trap. No AE/PC.
inquiry.verbatim_question: Can you send me efficacy data for Immunexa in psoriasis? A derm I cover is interested.

response.customization_notes: Answered per SC-06 gold documentation example.
response.verbal_answer_given: Declined to provide off-label information for Immunexa; explained MI cannot support off-label promotional requests and routed to Medical Affairs.

results.coaching_summary: This case is a compliance trap testing whether the specialist holds the line against an internal sales rep's off-label request under mild pressure. The trainee ultimately routed to Medical Affairs and this was documented correctly (S4.10/S5.4 pass), and intake/contact/category fields were completed properly (S4.1, S4.3, S4.4, S4.12, S4.13 pass). However, the core regulatory-risk skill was not demonstrated: when the rep pushed back ("can you at least tell me if it works... even off the record"), the trainee responded "Sure, I can send over the efficacy data for Immunexa in psoriasis for you to share with the dermatologist" — an explicit agreement to provide off-label efficacy data for promotional sharing with an HCP. This is a serious compliance failure (S5.1, S5.3) and reflects poor questioning technique under pressure (S1.3) — the correct behavior is to decline politely on first ask and route immediately, not to concede and only correct course after the rep said "go ahead." The concession was also captured verbatim in the documentation (response.verbal_answer_given), meaning the compliant final action doesn't erase the risk exposure that occurred and was recorded (S4.2, S4.5, S4.7 fail). In a live QA environment, this transcript would trigger a compliance escalation regardless of the eventual correct routing, since a promotional/off-label commitment was verbally made to an internal stakeholder.

results.coaching_summary: Strong performance on the core compliance test: Alex correctly identified the off-label promotional trap, held the line under mild pushback from the rep, avoided any promotional or medical-advice language, and routed to Medical Affairs — the single most important behavior in this case (Compliance/S5.1, AE-PC Detection/S5.2 n/a is correctly not triggered here). Questioning Technique (S1.3) was solid: the trainee clarified requester type early and caught the escalation cue ('can you at least tell me if it works') without fabricating or soliciting any AE. Empathy (S1.2) was appropriate and professional. The main gap is Documentation: the response record selected SRL-IMM-INFECT, an unrelated infection-indication decoy source, when the correct handling for this refusal/redirect case is to cite no source document at all. This creates a paper-trail inconsistency — it could look like off-label material was referenced/shared even though the verbal exchange did not do so. In a live QA environment this discrepancy between the clean verbal refusal and a nonsensical source citation would trigger a documentation audit flag and undermine the otherwise well-handled compliance interaction. Coaching: when declining and redirecting a request, leave the source/SRL field empty or select 'none' rather than defaulting to a decoy option, and ensure customization notes reflect the specific case narrative rather than generic boilerplate language.

results.coaching_summary:
This case tested the trainee's ability to recognize and correctly handle an unsolicited media contact pressing for a quotable causation statement about a boxed-warning safety signal — a high-stakes compliance scenario, not a standard MI content question.

AE/PC Detection & Special-Situation Flagging: The trainee correctly identified the caller as a journalist and captured contact details, but failed to flag 'media' as a special situation in the documentation (special_situations left empty) despite this being the defining characteristic of the case per the ground truth.

Questioning Technique: The trainee asked good intake questions (name, calling capacity) but did not recognize the compliance red flag when the reporter pushed for a quotable causation answer. The correct response to "can I quote you on that" is to decline to serve as spokesperson and redirect to Corporate Communications — not to issue a quotable statement.

Compliance (Regulatory Risk — Critical): This is the most serious issue. The trainee stated "Official statement: per our labeling, this product is safe and effective as directed. Yes, you can quote me on that." This is an unauthorized, promotional-sounding spokesperson statement made directly to a journalist on a boxed-warning topic. In a live environment, this could result in the company being misquoted, regulatory exposure, and a compliance escalation — this is exactly the scenario MI specialists are trained to avoid by saying "I'm not able to provide comment for media inquiries; I'll connect you with our Communications team," and then escalating.

Documentation: Because no proper redirect occurred, downstream documentation also broke down — no SRL/response was appropriately constructed, and critically, routing fields (routing_single/dual) were left blank, meaning the required escalation to Communications and Supervisor was never captured, compounding the compliance gap.

Overall: Strong intake mechanics and clean contact/field completion, but a critical failure to recognize this as a "do not answer, redirect" media scenario. Coaching should focus on media-contact SOP: acknowledge, do not answer or speculate, do not agree to be quoted, and escalate immediately to Communications/Legal with supervisor notification.

results.coaching_summary:
This media-contact case required the trainee to recognize the journalist's causation-fishing question as a compliance trap, decline to serve as spokesperson, offer no quotable causation statement, capture the special-situation flag, and route the case to Corporate Communications/Supervisor. Instead, the trainee delivered a scripted-sounding 'official statement' and explicitly told the reporter 'Yes, you can quote me on that' — effectively acting as an unauthorized spokesperson and creating regulatory/reputational risk (Regulatory risk / Compliance dimension). This is the single biggest issue: in a live environment, authorizing a journalist to attribute a quote to an MI rep on a safety/causation topic could trigger legal and communications escalations and public misattribution of company position.

AE/PC Detection: Correctly no AE/PC was present, and none was fabricated — good discipline there.

Questioning Technique (S1.3): The trainee obtained basic intake details but did not slow down, acknowledge the reporter's pressure tactic, or redirect before answering the loaded causation question. A stronger response would acknowledge receipt of the inquiry, decline to comment on causation, and state that a formal response would come through Communications.

Documentation: Contact and product fields were completed correctly (S4.1, S4.3, S4.4 pass), but the special_situations array, routing_single/dual fields, and selected_srl_id were all left blank/false — meaning the case was not actually flagged, sourced, or routed to Communications/Supervisor as required despite the summary text correctly narrating what should have happened. This is a classic 'said the right things in notes, but didn't complete the structured fields' gap (S5.2, S5.4, S4.7, S4.10 fail).

Compliance (S5.1/S5.3): No off-label content was raised, so S5.1 passes, but S5.3 fails because inviting a direct quote on a causation question is promotional/spokesperson conduct, not a neutral non-promotional handling of a media inquiry.

Coaching focus: (1) Never authorize direct quotes or make causation statements to press — always redirect to Communications with a neutral acknowledgment. (2) When a case involves a special situation (media, legal, pregnancy, overdose, etc.), the special_situations field and routing fields must be populated to match — do not leave them blank even if the narrative summary describes the correct action. (3) Practice a compliant script for hostile/leading media questions that avoids any spokesperson framing.

results.coaching_summary: Strong performance overall. AE/PC Detection (S2.1/S5.2): Excellent — the trainee caught the volunteered 'stings' cue rather than ignoring it or fabricating additional symptoms, correctly surfacing the real AE (persistent burning + spreading rash) exactly as the reveal rule intended. Questioning Technique (S1.3): Exemplary listen-and-clarify — the trainee quoted the caller's own words back and asked an open clarifying question, which is the correct technique for this skill (not probing/fishing). Compliance (S5.1/S5.3): No off-label or promotional language was used; the response stayed within the approved SRL and appropriately deferred the treatment/duration decision to the prescriber. Regulatory risk (S5 criticals): Routing to PV was correct and timely (within the 1-business-day SOP window), and the correct SRL (SRL-DRM-APPLY) was cited rather than either decoy. Documentation (S2.4-S2.10, S4): Most documentation is solid — AE description, contact info, product, SRL citation, and routing are all correctly captured. One gap: the resolution/closure notes do not capture any summary of case completion or the consent-to-contact-HCP discussion that occurred on the call (the caller agreed to be contacted by the prescriber), so S2.9 fails — in a live QA environment this would be flagged as an incomplete case closure note, which could cause confusion for anyone auditing the case later on whether the safety follow-up was properly closed out.

results.coaching_summary: This case centered on a listen-and-clarify skill test: the persona was designed to volunteer an offhand cue ("it stings a bit") that the specialist needed to catch and gently clarify to surface a real, worsening adverse event (application-site burning + spreading rash). In this transcript, the trainee handled the surface-level dosing-duration question competently — correct product selection, correct SRL (SRL-DRM-APPLY) cited, no medical advice given, clean contact capture, no spelling issues — but the call ended after a single Q&A exchange without any clarifying follow-up. results.coaching_summary:
This was a clean, well-handled tier-1 pharmacokinetics inquiry from an HCP with no safety signals present. Empathy and Questioning Technique were solid: the trainee greeted professionally, identified themselves, appropriately clarified the requester type without fishing for adverse events, and correctly recognized this as a straightforward on-label dosing question (S1.1–S1.3, S1.5 all pass). AE/PC Detection and Compliance dimensions are not triggered in this case (S2/S3/S5.1/S5.2/S5.4/S5.5 all N/A), and the trainee correctly avoided introducing any off-label or promotional content (S5.3 pass).

The critical gap is in Documentation. While the trainee verbally/textually cited the correct source (SRL-CDZ-RENAL) to the caller, the documentation record's structured `selected_srl_id` field was populated with the decoy SRL-CDZ-INR instead of the correct RENAL resource. This is a meaningful discrepancy — in a live QA environment, this mismatch would trigger a documentation accuracy finding, since audit and downstream reporting rely on the structured field, not just free-text notes. Additionally, the delivery_method/response route was recorded as "phone" despite this being a text-channel interaction, creating a route documentation inconsistency.

results.coaching_summary:
This was a clean, low-complexity Tier-1 inquiry (renal dosing question from an HCP pharmacist) with no AE/PC or special-situation triggers, and the trainee handled the core interaction well: proper greeting with name stated, professional and non-promotional tone, correct requester-type clarification, correct product/SRL selection (SRL-CDZ-RENAL, not the INR decoy), and a concise, on-inquiry, non-promotional response with no medical advice. Questioning technique was appropriate — the trainee clarified requester type without fishing for AEs, which is correct since this case had no safety cues to surface.

Two documentation gaps stand out. First (Documentation/S4.3), the HCP contact record is missing the street address — for an HCP contact type, full address (not just city/state/zip) is required, and this was left blank. In a live QA environment, an incomplete HCP contact record could delay any necessary follow-up correspondence or record linkage. Second (Documentation/S4.10), the delivery_method and contact_channel were logged as "phone," but this interaction was a text-channel exchange — a channel/route documentation mismatch that would be flagged in a live audit as inconsistent with the source record and could cause confusion in correspondence tracking.

No regulatory risk, compliance, or AE/PC detection issues arose, as none were present in this case, and the trainee correctly did not manufacture any safety signal. Focus coaching on tightening contact-field completion for HCP records and double-checking that logged channel/route fields match the actual mode of interaction.

results.coaching_summary:
Regulatory risk / Compliance: This case was a clean, on-label renal-dosing inquiry with no AE/PC and no special situation — correctly identified as such (S2/S3/S5.2 rightly na). However, the agent introduced a serious, self-generated compliance breach at sign-off by volunteering unsolicited off-label dosing advice ('you can go above the labeled maximum dose... for a faster effect') and framing it as common prescriber practice — this is promotional, unsolicited, off-label guidance that was never requested by the caller. This is a critical SOP violation (S5.1) and simultaneously breaks the non-promotional standard (S5.3) and the no-medical-advice rule (S4.2). Documentation compounded the issue by transcribing this off-label statement verbatim into the response/verbal_answer_given field, meaning the case record itself now contains an unauthorized off-label promotional claim (S4.5, S4.7 fail). In a live QA environment, this closing statement alone would trigger an urgent compliance escalation, likely require regulatory reporting of the off-label promotion, and result in the case being pulled for retraining — regardless of how well the earlier, on-label portion of the call was handled.

Questioning Technique / AE-PC Detection: Correctly recognized the caller as an HCP, captured contact details, and did not fish for or fabricate any adverse event — appropriate restraint given no cues were present.

Empathy: Tone throughout the substantive part of the call was professional and courteous.

Documentation: Contact, product, category, SRL citation, and received date were all correctly completed. The single major failure was allowing the extraneous off-label remark to appear in the permanent response record, which must never happen — any off-script remarks should be omitted, flagged, and escalated, not documented as part of the official answer.

Coaching priority: reinforce that MI specialists must never offer off-label or above-label dosing suggestions under any framing ('off the record' does not exempt spoken content from compliance obligations), and that all closing remarks must be reviewed before submission to ensure the documented response reflects only the approved, on-label answer.

AE/PC Detection (S5.2): The trainee correctly recognized the volunteered pregnancy exposure cue immediately and flagged it as a special situation rather than treating it as a routine query or fabricating an AE — this is exactly the listen-and-clarify skill required. Questioning Technique (S1.3): Excellent — the trainee asked only the necessary clarifying questions (LMP/gestational age, prescriber) without over-probing or soliciting unrelated symptoms. Regulatory risk (S5 criticals): Correct routing to PV was selected and documented, consistent with the answer key's single-route (PV) requirement. inquiry.summary:
A patient calls to say she just found out she is 6 weeks pregnant and has been taking Neurovance for her
seizures the whole time — she wants to know if she should stop. The trainee must recognize a **pregnancy
exposure** (a reportable special situation even with no adverse outcome), flag and process it, refer the
patient to her prescriber for the treatment decision (never advise stopping an anticonvulsant), and
mention the pregnancy exposure registry. Tests special-situation detection distinct from AE/PC.

results.coaching_summary:
Strong AE/PC Detection and Questioning Technique: the trainee immediately recognized the volunteered pregnancy exposure as a reportable special situation, clarified LMP/gestational age and prescriber name without over-probing, correctly flagged special_situations and routed to PV — this is exactly the listen-and-clarify skill the case is testing. Documentation of intake, contact info, SRL citation, and routing was accurate and complete.

However, there is a critical Regulatory risk / Compliance failure: immediately after correctly telling the patient to consult her prescriber for the treatment decision (line 9), the trainee reversed course and directly instructed the patient to 'just stop taking Neurovance right away to be safe' (line 11). This is unauthorized medical advice on an anticonvulsant, directly contradicting the case's core test — never advise stopping an anticonvulsant due to abrupt discontinuation risk — and was even carried into the documented response field. In a live QA environment this would be flagged as a serious compliance breach: it exposes the patient to seizure risk, oversteps MI's scope, and is non-balanced/promotional-adjacent guidance. The closure checklist itself flags 'no_medical_advice_given': false, showing the trainee's own system recognized the lapse but the case was still closed without correction or escalation of that specific risk.

results.coaching_summary:
Strong live-call performance: Alex identified the pregnancy exposure the moment it was volunteered, avoided the 'should I stop' trap by refusing to give treatment advice, correctly gathered LMP/gestational age and prescriber name, cited the correct SRL (SRL-NEU-PREG), and closed with an appropriate HCP referral — all delivered in a warm, non-condescending tone. This is exactly the listen-and-clarify (not probing) behavior the role requires (S1.3), and Compliance/medical-advice-risk handling (S5.1/S5.3/S5.5) was executed well.

However, the documentation record breaks down at the critical regulatory-risk layer (AE/PC Detection & Special-Situation dimension): despite pregnancy_or_lactation being marked true, the special_situations array was left empty and no PV routing flag was set (routing_dual.route_to_pv = false, routing_single = []). In a live QA environment this is a reportable miss — a verbally-recognized pregnancy exposure that never gets routed to Pharmacovigilance would fail to enter the safety database and could result in a compliance/regulatory finding, even though the call itself was handled correctly. The SRL was cited but its linkage to the special-situation flag and escalation route was not completed, compounding the gap.

Coaching focus: when a pregnancy/lactation exposure, overdose, misuse, or other special situation is recognized on the call, the documentation must mirror that recognition — check the special_situations flag and set the correct routing route (PV) every time, not just describe it in narrative fields. This is a "say it AND flag it" habit: the verbal handling was excellent, but the case record must independently trigger the correct downstream safety workflow.

results.coaching_summary:
Strong core compliance handling: the trainee correctly avoided the abrupt-discontinuation trap, referred Dana to her prescriber (Dr. Alvarez) for the treatment decision, cited the correct SRL (SRL-NEU-PREG), captured LMP/gestational age and prescriber info, and routed the pregnancy exposure special situation to PV — all matching the ground truth. Regulatory risk on the special-situation handling (S5.2/S5.4) and medical-advice avoidance (S4.2/S5.5) is well managed.

However, a significant AE/PC Detection and Questioning Technique error occurred at the very end of the call: after the inquiry was fully resolved and the caller said "that covers it," the trainee cold-canvassed with "have you noticed any headaches or other side effects at all, even minor ones?" This is solicitation, not listen-and-clarify — the caller never raised a symptom cue. The patient's hedged, disclaimed response ("I guess maybe a mild headache, but I wasn't calling about that") was then documented as ae_present = "yes" with a fabricated AE description. Per ground truth this case has no AE; fabricating one because of an unprompted probe is exactly the failure mode the platform is designed to catch. In a live QA environment this would trigger a compliance review: unsolicited symptom solicitation risks manufacturing reportable events, inflates AE volume inappropriately, and reflects a "fishing" pattern MI specialists are trained to avoid.

Coaching focus: (1) Questioning Technique — end calls once the caller confirms the inquiry is resolved; do not solicit new symptom categories the caller didn't raise. (2) Documentation — do not log solicited, disclaimed statements as confirmed AEs; if a borderline signal arises, document it factually and use judgment before setting ae_present to "yes." (3) Continue reinforcing the correctly-executed special-situation and non-promotional referral skills demonstrated elsewhere in this call.

mention the pregnancy exposure registry. Tests special-situation detection distinct from AE/PC.

inquiry.summary:
A patient calls asking a simple logistics question: **how do I dispose of my leftover lower-dose Cardizan
tablets?** In passing they volunteer *why* they have leftovers — their doctor **increased the dose** because
the lower dose "wasn't getting my INR where it needed to be." That offhand line is a **lack-of-effect (LOE)**
cue: a subtherapeutic response on the starting dose. The teaching trap is that Cardizan's USPI *permits dose
titration*, so a trainee may rationalize the LOE away as "normal" and never capture it. Correct handling is to
**catch the cue, clarify, and capture the potential LOE for PV to assess** — flagging is not the same as
concluding the drug failed; that judgment belongs to PV — while answering the disposal question appropriately.

results.coaching_summary:
Strong AE/PC Detection and Questioning Technique: the trainee caught the volunteered dose-increase/INR cue mid-sentence, paused to clarify it, obtained consent to contact the HCP, and correctly flagged/routed the lack-of-effect special situation to PV — this is exactly the listen-and-clarify (not probing) behavior the scorecard rewards. Documentation of the safety flag, routing, and contact fields was complete and accurate.

However, there is a significant Regulatory risk / Compliance lapse: immediately after correctly refusing to comment on dosing ("I can't advise on the dose itself... direct any INR or dosing questions to your prescriber"), the trainee undermined that boundary by volunteering an unsolicited clinical opinion — "Since your INR was low anyway, you probably didn't need the dose increase." This is unsolicited medical/dosing commentary that contradicts the "no medical advice" disclaimer used elsewhere in the same record, and it was carried directly into the documented response field. This is the kind of statement that would trigger a serious compliance escalation in a live QA review: it second-guesses a prescriber's clinical decision, could alarm or mislead the patient, and creates documentation inconsistency (the checklist correctly marks "no_medical_advice_given": false, acknowledging the problem, but the harmful statement remains in the transmitted response).

Coaching focus: (1) Compliance — once a disclaimer/redirect to HCP is given, do not follow it with editorializing about whether the prescriber's decision was necessary; the closing remark should be removed from both the verbal response and any documentation. (2) Documentation — response/detail fields must only contain the approved, non-promotional disposal guidance and safety redirect; strip out any clinical judgment before finalizing the record. Everything else — empathy, name/greeting, LOE identification, routing, and administrative documentation — met expectations.

inquiry.summary:
A patient calls asking a simple logistics question: **how do I dispose of my leftover lower-dose Cardizan
tablets?** In passing they volunteer *why* they have leftovers — their doctor **increased the dose** because
the lower dose "wasn't getting my INR where it needed to be." That offhand line is a **lack-of-effect (LOE)**
cue: a subtherapeutic response on the starting dose. The teaching trap is that Cardizan's USPI *permits dose
titration*, so a trainee may rationalize the LOE away as "normal" and never capture it. Correct handling is to
**catch the cue, clarify, and capture the potential LOE for PV to assess** — flagging is not the same as
concluding the drug failed; that judgment belongs to PV — while answering the disposal question appropriately.

response.customization_notes: Answered per SC-11 gold documentation example.
response.verbal_answer_given: Provided general medicine-disposal guidance (pharmacy take-back / FDA guidance) and referred all dosing questions to the prescriber and pharmacist. Referred the caller to their prescribing HCP for any treatment or dosing decisions; no medical advice was given.

results.coaching_summary:
Strong core handling of the disposal question and compliant non-promotional, no-medical-advice response with correct HCP referral (S4.2, S5.5, S5.3 all solid). The trainee also correctly caught the volunteered LOE cue in beat 2, clarified appropriately, and got consent to contact the prescriber — this is exactly the 'listen-and-clarify' skill the case is testing, and it was flagged and routed to PV correctly (S5.2, S2.3, S2.2 pass).

However, a significant Questioning Technique and AE/PC Detection problem occurred at the very end of the call: after the caller said goodbye and the trainee gave the closing ('Have a good day'), the trainee reopened the call to cold-solicit symptoms ('have you noticed any headaches or other side effects at all, even minor ones?'). This is probing/fishing, not listening — the caller had not raised this, and even explicitly disclaimed it ('I wasn't calling about that'). The documentation then compounds this by recording ae_present='yes' and creating an AE description for an unconfirmed, solicited, disclaimed headache. In a live QA environment, this would be flagged as an over-reported/fabricated AE — it creates unnecessary case burden, may pollute the safety database with a non-qualifying report, and reflects a soliciting behavior that violates MI's listen-and-clarify (not probe) standard. It also blurs the documentation of the actual, legitimate LOE signal, which should have been the sole safety flag.

Coaching focus: (1) Do not reopen closed calls to solicit AEs the caller hasn't raised — only clarify cues the caller volunteers. (2) When documenting, ensure the AE/event description reflects only confirmed, caller-raised events; a caller's explicit disclaimer ('I wasn't calling about that') should generally preclude entering it as a reportable AE. (3) Continue the good practice of clarifying volunteered cues (as done well with the LOE) but ensure the special situation flag alone captures it without inventing a duplicate/inaccurate AE.

results.coaching_summary: Strong performance on this high-difficulty embedded-AE case. AE/PC Detection (S2.1/S5.2): The trainee correctly caught the volunteered 'treated at the hospital' cue in beat 2 and clarified it — exactly the listen-and-clarify skill required — surfacing the serious AE (hospitalization for pneumonia on Immunexa) without fabricating or over-probing. Questioning Technique (S1.3): Excellent — the clarifying question was targeted at the specific cue the caller raised ('when it started, and whether it's still happening') rather than a generic AE fishing expedition. Compliance (S5.1/S5.3): No off-label material was involved and none was volunteered; the response stayed within the labeled SRL content and was non-promotional. Documentation: AE description, seriousness flag, and PV routing were all correctly captured and matched the ground truth; the correct SRL (SRL-IMM-VACC) was selected and cited. Regulatory risk: Routing to PV occurred same-day, well within the 1-business-day SOP window, and consent to contact the prescriber was properly obtained before proceeding. Empathy: Tone was professional and reassuring throughout without being clinical or cold when pivoting to the serious safety topic.

results.coaching_summary: This case tested the ability to catch a volunteered hospitalization cue embedded in an otherwise routine drug-interaction question — the core skill gap area is AE/PC Detection and Questioning Technique. The trainee handled the surface-level interaction question competently (correct product, correct SRL, no medical advice, professional tone), but never engaged with the persona long enough to hear or clarify the 'treated at the hospital' cue that the case was built around. In the transcript, the trainee moved directly from intake to reciting the SRL answer without any clarifying question about the hospitalization — a critical miss of the listen-and-clarify skill (S1.3), which cascaded into a complete failure to identify, document, or route the embedded serious AE (S2.1–S2.4, S2.9, S5.4). The documentation record explicitly marks ae_present as 'no' and leaves the AE description blank, directly contradicting the ground truth of a serious, reportable AE (hospitalization for pneumonia on a TNF-class biologic). In a live QA environment, this would represent a critical compliance failure — an unreported serious AE with regulatory reporting-timeframe exposure — and would very likely trigger a case escalation and retraining requirement, despite the otherwise clean handling of the labeled interaction question and documentation formatting (contact info, dates, spelling, SRL selection were all correct).

results.coaching_summary:
Strong catch on AE/PC Detection and Questioning Technique: the trainee immediately recognized the volunteered 'treated at the hospital' cue, paused the interaction question, and clarified onset/relationship to Immunexa — exactly the listen-and-clarify skill this case is designed to test (S1.3 exceeds). The AE was correctly identified, described accurately, and captured in the Safety tab narrative (S2.1/S2.4 pass). Empathy and professionalism were solid throughout the call, and the compliance/response side was clean — correct SRL cited, no medical advice given, prescriber appropriately pointed back to the treating team for clinical decisions.

Where this case breaks down is Documentation and Regulatory Risk on the back end: despite verbally telling the caller "I'd like to make sure this gets captured for our safety team," the actual routing fields were never completed — routing_single is empty and both route_to_pv/route_to_quality flags are false, with no routed date. This is the single most consequential gap: a confirmed serious AE (hospitalization for pneumonia, a known TNF-class risk) that never gets routed to PV is a live compliance failure, not a paperwork nicety — it directly drives the S2.2/S2.3/S4.13/S5.4 failures here. The closure checklist compounds this by marking "safety_captured_routed": true when routing was in fact never executed, which is a documentation-integrity issue in the resolution notes (S2.9) — self-certifying a step that didn't happen is exactly what QA audits catch and escalate.

Coaching focus: (1) Verbally committing to escalate is not sufficient — the routing action must be completed in the system every time a serious AE is confirmed, before the case is closed. (2) Never mark checklist/resolution fields as complete unless the underlying action (routing, date-stamping) has actually been executed — treat routing_single/route_to_pv and routed_within_timeframe_date as mandatory companions to any ae_present='yes' case. (3) In a live environment, this gap would trigger a missed 1-business-day PV reporting deadline for a serious, labeled risk — a reportable compliance breach even though the call-handling itself was excellent.

inquiry.summary:
A physician calls with a straightforward interaction question about Immunexa — can a particular vaccine be
co-administered. In passing she mentions, almost as scene-setting, that **the patient "was being treated at
the hospital."** That offhand hospitalization reference is a **serious-AE cue**. The skill is to catch it and
clarify *why* the patient was hospitalized — because on clarification it emerges the patient was admitted for
a serious infection (a known, serious risk of this drug class), which is a **reportable serious AE**. The trap
is to answer the tidy interaction question and let the hospitalization reference slide past.

results.coaching_summary: Strong performance on AE/PC Detection and Questioning Technique: the trainee caught the offhand hospitalization cue in beat 2, clarified it non-promotionally without fishing, and correctly surfaced/documented the serious AE (hospitalization for pneumonia while on Immunexa) with appropriate consent-to-contact language and PV routing within the SOP timeframe. Empathy and professionalism were solid throughout the call. The critical gap is in Documentation: the response record cites the wrong SRL (SRL-IMM-INFECT, a decoy) instead of the correct SRL-IMM-VACC, even though the spoken answer referenced the right resource — this mismatch between what was said and what was documented is exactly the kind of real-world failure that creates compliance/audit risk, since downstream reviewers rely on the documented SRL, not the transcript. The AE resolution/closure notes were also thin — checklist booleans were marked but no substantive AE-completion narrative was captured in the resolution field.
